hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the CLA C118 coupé — from the 1.3-litre turbo to the 2.0-litre AMG monsters. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the M139 that redefined the hot-hatch engine. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the CLA C118 coupé specifically — petrol, diesel, mild-hybrid and plug-in hybrid across the 2019–2025 production run.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 is the second-generation CLA four-door coupé, launched in 2019 as the successor to the C117. Built on the MFA2 platform shared with the W177 A-Class and W247 B-Class, the C118 brought a longer wheelbase, wider track and a completely new interior architecture. Engine choice spans the Renault-sourced M282 1.3-litre turbo petrol (shared with the A-Class and Renault Mégane), the Mercedes-developed M260 2.0-litre turbo petrol, the M139 2.0-litre turbo (the most powerful series-production four-cylinder in the world at launch), and the OM608/OM654 diesel range. A plug-in hybrid (CLA 250 e) joined the range in 2020.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz MFA2 (C118 coupé variant) • Predecessor: C117 CLA • Successor: — (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: X118 Shooting Brake.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C118 coupé attracts strong specialist interest in the UK used market, with M282 petrol and OM654 diesel enquiries dominating pre-purchase inspections [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The C118's engine range splits into three distinct reliability tiers. At the top sits the M260 2.0-litre turbo (CLA 250, CLA 35 AMG) — a Mercedes-developed unit with strong durability and fewer reported issues than its smaller sibling. The OM654 2.0-litre diesel (CLA 200 d/220 d) is the pick of the diesel range — a modern, efficient unit with good real-world economy and proven longevity in the wider Mercedes lineup. The M282 1.3-litre turbo (CLA 180/200) sits in the middle — a Renault-sourced engine that's generally reliable but has documented timing chain and injector sensitivity, particularly on higher-mileage examples. The M139 (CLA 45/45 S) is exceptional — the most powerful four-cylinder production engine ever, but its complexity and extreme tune make it the most expensive to maintain outside warranty. The OM608 1.5-litre diesel (CLA 180 d) is the least recommended — a Renault K9K-based unit with injector and DPF sensitivity that doesn't justify its economy advantage over the OM654. For most buyers, the M260 petrol or OM654 diesel represent the sweet spot between performance, economy and running costs. Who it's for: buyers who want the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Good Value — AMG performance, reasonable running costs

economicsRule.title: The C118 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£6,000 M260 recon against a £12,000–£20,000 C118 sits at 18–50% of the car's value — well within Mercedes-Benz's 70% threshold. Even a £6,000–£10,000 M139 recon on a £25,000+ CLA 45 sits at 24–40% — safe. The C118's relatively strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable, with the exception of very high-mileage CLA 180 d examples where the OM608 recon may approach the car's value.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C118 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM654 diesel — strong fuel economy, proven durability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C118 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M139 (CLA 45/45 S) — extreme tune, extreme running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C118 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLA 250 (M260) — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: M282 Timing Chain Stretch (Renault-Sourced Engine)
problems.rootCause: The M282 is a Renault-Nissan-Mitsubishi Alliance engine (codenamed HR13DDT). Like many Renault-sourced engines, the timing chain tensioner can wear prematurely, leading to chain stretch and rattle on cold start. The chain is located at the rear of the engine, making replacement more labour-intensive than a typical front-chain design.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on a CLA 180 or CLA 200, get the timing chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare C118 M282 timing chain repair quotes →

problems.title: OM654 AdBlue System & EGR Cooler Issues
problems.rootCause: The OM654's AdBlue system (SCR) can suffer from crystallisation in the injector and NOx sensor failures. EGR cooler leaks are also documented, particularly on higher-mileage examples. These are common modern diesel issues rather than design flaws specific to the OM654.
problems.recommendation: Have the AdBlue system and EGR cooler inspected at every major service — a £400–£800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,800–£3,000 replacement that follows a full system failure.

cta.label: Compare C118 OM654 AdBlue repair quotes →

problems.title: M260 Water Pump & Cooling System Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M260's water pump and thermostat housing can develop leaks, leading to coolant loss and potential overheating. This is a well-documented issue across the M260 family in A-Class, CLA and GLA applications.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels regularly — a £400–£800 water pump replacement is cheap insurance against a £2,000+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare C118 M260 water pump repair quotes →

problems.title: M139 Turbocharger & Cooling System Stress
problems.affectedModels: CLA 45 AMG, CLA 45 S AMG (M139.980) — 2019–2025 production.
problems.rootCause: The M139 is the most powerful series-production four-cylinder engine ever, producing up to 421 bhp from 2.0 litres. This extreme specific output (over 210 bhp per litre) places significant stress on the turbocharger, cooling system and injectors — particularly on cars that are tracked or driven hard.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a CLA 45 or 45 S, full service history is essential — a £1,000–£1,800 preventative turbo inspection is far cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare C118 M139 turbo repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: C118 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned C118 engine typically costs between £2,500 and £10,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the volume M282 petrol sits at £2,500–£4,500 and the OM654 diesel at £3,200–£5,500. The high-performance M139 in the CLA 45/45 S ranges from £6,000–£10,000. Fitting adds 8–18 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £520–£1,170 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The M260 2.0-litre turbo petrol (CLA 250, CLA 35 AMG) is widely regarded as the most durable C118 engine — a Mercedes-developed unit with strong reliability and fewer documented issues than the Renault-sourced M282. The OM654 2.0-litre diesel is the pick of the diesel range — modern, efficient and proven in the wider Mercedes lineup.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz CLA C118?
items.answer: Timing chain stretch on the M282 1.3-litre turbo (CLA 180/200, Renault-sourced), AdBlue system and EGR cooler issues on the OM654 diesel (CLA 200 d/220 d), water pump and cooling system leaks on the M260 (CLA 250/CLA 35), and turbocharger/cooling system stress on the M139 (CLA 45/45 S) are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 reliable?
items.answer: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The M260 petrol and OM654 diesel are generally reliable with routine maintenance. The M282 (Renault-sourced) requires careful timing chain history checking. The M139 is spectacular but demands specialist attention and deeper pockets. Overall, a well-maintained C118 is a solid used proposition.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but a high-mileage M282 without documented timing chain replacement is a purchase we consistently advise against. The OM608 1.5-litre diesel (CLA 180 d) is the least recommended — a Renault K9K-based unit with injector and DPF sensitivity that doesn't justify its economy advantage over the OM654.

items.question: How do I know if my C118 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Cold-start rattle (M282 timing chain), AdBlue warning lights (OM654), coolant loss (M260 water pump), turbocharger whine or loss of power (M139), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a C118 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 CLA 200, a £4,000 M282 recon is 27% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £6,000 example, the same recon is 67% — borderline but still within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. For the M139, the £6,000+ recon on a £25,000+ CLA 45 is just 24–40% — safe.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 engines last?
items.answer: The M260 petrol and OM654 diesel can comfortably exceed 120,000 miles with proper servicing. The M282's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can reach 100,000+ miles. The M139, while robust for its output, will demand more frequent maintenance, particularly on tracked cars.

items.question: What is the difference between the C118 and X118?
items.answer: Both are second-generation CLA models sharing the MFA2 platform and most engines. The C118 is the 4-door coupé (2019–2025, this page), while the X118 is the Shooting Brake estate variant (2019–present). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the X118 generation page for specifics.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz CLA C118 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on M282/M260/M139/OM608/OM654 units. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
